Learning Python From a JS Angle (23): Classes — and Why JavaScript Isn't Quite OOP
This one's a bit of a breather. We're just going to have a quick, low-key chat about Python's
syntax. Nothing too heavy, I promise.
Python is an object-oriented language (Object-oriented programming, usually shortened to OOP). So... what does object-oriented actually mean? And is JavaScript object-oriented too?

Honestly, this question bugged me for a long time. Some people swear JavaScript is object-oriented, others insist it isn't, so before we touch Python let me untangle it a little. Instead of talking myself in circles, let's just pull up some docs. Here's how Wikipedia describes "object-oriented programming":

It may contain data, attributes, code, and methods. An object is an instance of a class. OOP treats objects as the basic unit of a program, packaging code and data together to improve reusability, flexibility, and extensibility. The code inside an object can access and frequently modify the data associated with that object. In object-oriented programming, a computer program is designed as a collection of objects that relate to one another.
Yeah, I'm guessing that read like word soup. It's pretty stiffly worded:
But you might be thinking, "Object-oriented... so this is related to objects, right?"
And the answer is "Yep, very much so." It's tied closely to objects. On top of that, object-oriented development usually starts by declaring a class — that's the
is basically an abstract object. For example, you might have a Dog class, and that Dog class holds all the basic traits a dog has. The shape of it might look something like this:
language-cmd shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

class Dog
  public methods:
    bark()
    claws:
  private methods:
    coat color:
    breed:
    tail length:
    name:
    scientific name:
(I would never admit I came up with that class by staring at my own dog.)

And from that Dog class we can keep inheriting downward to give each one its own specifics — coat color is brown, breed is mixed, tail length is none, and so on. So you can also think of object-oriented programming as a blueprint concept.
By that logic, JavaScript is object-oriented too, then? Basically yes — JavaScript is object-oriented, but it's
object-oriented. I know that already sounds like a tongue twister, so let's just look at some examples.
Here's some Java, just thrown together quickly for comparison:

Do you know the difference between Java and JavaScript?
The thing is, you can see Java uses
to abstract a thing, a concept, and that
can be inherited over and over.

But in the JavaScript example we just saw, JS can do something pretty similar too. So doesn't that make JavaScript object-oriented? In practice, it's a little awkward to call JavaScript straight-up OOP — it fits better as OOJS (Object-oriented JavaScript), since JavaScript is a prototype-based language. For a much better write-up, check out the
chapter on MDN. What I'm doing here is just a quick chat — I'm not planning to go too deep on this.

Can't tell what's Python and what's JavaScript anymore
So how do we instantiate the class we just declared? Not sure what instantiating means? In short, you can think of it as giving that abstract thing a sense of life. Let's see how it's done:

class Home:
  father = 'jack'

myHome = Home()
print(myHome.father) # jack

Next, similar to Java, we write our method functions inside the
. But heads up here: remember to pass
into the method, otherwise you won't be able to access the
And if you want to modify an attribute on Home, that's dead easy too — same as modifying any object property:
There's also deleting attributes and methods from a class, and so on:

class Home:
  def __init__ (self, father):
    self.father = father
  def myFather(self):
    print('my father is ' + self.father)

myHome = Home('jack')
print(myHome.father) # jack
myHome.myFather() # my father is jack

myHome.father = 'Ray'
print(myHome.father) # Ray

del myHome.father
del myHome
Just note that once you've deleted something, it can't be called anymore.

A quick, low-key chat about Python's class syntax. Along the way we untangle a question that bugged me for ages: is JavaScript actually object-oriented? Spoiler — kind of, but not the way you think. Then we build a Python class step by step, meet __init__ and self, and see how close it all feels to JS.
